body: The hand-drawing tip is underrated. I did the same thing for B-trees and it clicked way faster than any video explanation.
Wish I'd read this before my exam last semester. Failed it and had to resit, mostly because I was memorizing instead of understanding. Resit went much better.
The sleep thing is real. I tracked my exam scores vs sleep the night before and the correlation was uncomfortable to look at.
Which ThinkCentre model? I've been eyeing the M710q but the M920q might be worth the extra cost for the newer chipset.
The M920q is a great pick. I got one last year for 1200 and it's been running non-stop. The NVMe slot is a huge upgrade over the M710q's SATA-only setup.
The Q&A tool is genuinely something that would improve lectures. Have you considered making it public or open-sourcing it?
Was at the hackathon. The navigation app demo was impressive — worked on the floor plans in real time. Deserved first place.
The kickstart.nvim recommendation is solid. I made the mistake of starting from scratch and spent a week fighting Lua config instead of learning the editor.
Still not convinced it's worth the initial cost in productivity. What's your current plugin count? I feel like most setups end up as VS Code with more typing.
The 'using it as a tutor' framing is exactly right. I spent months using it to write code I should be writing myself, and my actual skills were going backwards.
Curious which model you find most useful for explaining concepts — I've been bouncing between a few and the quality varies a lot by subject.
Been using this for years. The heap sort visualization finally made the heapify step make sense to me after many failed attempts with textbooks.
The shell tools lecture alone is worth it. I was writing terrible bash scripts for two years before watching that and realized I was doing everything wrong.
Recommended this to my entire study group. The metaprogramming and security lectures are particularly good — things I had no idea I was missing.
CodeCrafters is excellent in general. Also did the 'Build Your Own Git' course and understood Git internals better after that than after two years of using it.
